context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 3

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 26th February, 2024
Days Covered: 04 and 05 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Sorting by Reversals [15 points]

We have a permutation of the numbers {0, 1, ..., n − 1}, and we'd like to manipulate it so it becomes sorted in increasing order. For this problem, we're only allowed to make reversal moves: from a list (a0, a1, ..., a_{n−1}), we can pick a pair of indices i < j and reverse the entire subsequence from a_i to a_j:
    (a0, ..., a_{i−1}, a_i, a_{i+1}, ..., a_{j−1}, a_j, a_{j+1}, ..., a_{n−1})
  → (a0, ..., a_{i−1}, a_j, a_{j−1}, ..., a_{i+1}, a_i, a_{j+1}, ..., a_{n−1}).
If (i, j) is an inverted pair (i.e., i < j but a_i > a_j), call this move a good reversal.

In this problem we'll show that if we start with any permutation (x0, ..., x_{n−1}) of {0, ..., n − 1} and keep making good reversal moves, then no matter which ones we choose, we will eventually terminate with a sorted sequence.

(a) [3 pts] Describe this scenario as a state machine, where the states are the permutations of {0, 1, ..., n − 1}. What are the transitions? The start state?
(b) [3 pts] What are the final states? How do you know? Conclude partial correctness: if an execution of the state machine terminates, then the list must be sorted.
(c) [6 pts] In lecture, we proved termination of swap-sort by proving that the number of inverted pairs strictly decreases as we make swaps. This is not true for this problem! For example, (9, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 0) can transition to (0, 8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 9), but the former has 17 inverted pairs while the latter has 28 inverted pairs.
Even so, please find a derived variable for this problem that (i) has only nonnegative integer values and (ii) is strictly decreasing. Be sure to prove that your derived variable satisfies both properties.
    Hint: Think of (a0, ..., a_{n−1}) as digits of a base-n number.
(d) [3 pts] Use parts (b) and (c) to prove total correctness: every execution must eventually terminate with a fully sorted list.


Problem 2. A Fibonacci Sum [10 points]

Recall that the Fibonacci sequence is given by F0 = 0, F1 = 1, and Fn = F_{n−1} + F_{n−2} for n > 1, so the sequence starts with 0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, ....

(a) [8 pts] Use the perturbation method to find the closed form of the sum
    S = Σ_{n=1}^∞ Fn / c^n,
where c = 10^5. You may assume the sum converges.
    Hint: Try comparing S with S/c.
(b) [2 pts] Verify your answer with WolframAlpha (www.wolframalpha.com) or some other high-precision calculator, recalling that c = 10^5. What are the first 50 digits of your result? What is the pattern and does it make sense?


Problem 3. Integral Method [15 points]

Let f(x) := x / (5 + x)^3, and let S_n := Σ_{x=n}^∞ f(x). In this problem, we will use the integral method to estimate the infinite sum S0 to an accuracy of 1/200. In other words, we will find bounds u and v such that u ≤ S0 ≤ v and |v − u| ≤ 1/200.
Feel free to use a calculator (e.g., WolframAlpha) for algebraic/numerical computations, but be sure to justify all of your reasoning.

(a) [2 pts] Explain briefly why applying the integral method directly to S0 will not work.
(b) [3 pts] Find the smallest index i such that the sum S_i can be approximated by applying the integral method directly.
(c) [4 pts] Use your answer from the previous part to find upper and lower bounds for S0. What accuracy does your approximation achieve? (I.e., what is the difference between your upper and lower bounds?)
(d) [6 pts] Find the smallest index j such that the sum S_j can be approximated to an accuracy of 1/200 by applying the integral method directly. Use your answer to find u and v (expressed as rational numbers) such that u ≤ S0 ≤ v and |v − u| ≤ 1/200.
    Hint: j should be a single digit integer.


Problem 4. Some Sums [10 points]

Find the closed form of each of the summations below.

(a) [5 pts]  Σ_{i=0}^n (5^i − 7^i) / 13^i
(b) [5 pts]  Σ_{i=1}^n Σ_{j=1}^k (3^j − i)

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m pretty lost on Problem 1, especially part (c). I sort of get the reversal move, but I don’t see how to come up with a decreasing derived variable, and the base-\(n\) hint is confusing me.
exchanges.tutor: Let's start simple. If you treat the permutation (a0, a1, ..., a_{n−1}) as the digits of a base-n number, what number does the sorted permutation (0, 1, 2, ..., n−1) represent, and what number does, say, (n−1, n−2, ..., 1, 0) represent? Which one is larger?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting on part (c) and feeling lost. Before diving into the hint, I should check their understanding of the basics first. I'll ask a targeted Socratic question to get them thinking about what the base-n representation of a permutation actually looks like, and what happens to that number when a good reversal is made. This keeps the scaffolding minimal and lets them discover the idea themselves.

exchanges.student: I’m not fully sure, but I think \((a_0,\dots,a_{n-1})\) would mean \(a_0 n^{n-1}+a_1 n^{n-2}+\cdots+a_{n-1}\). So \((0,1,2,\dots,n-1)\) would be \(0\cdot n^{n-1}+1\cdot n^{n-2}+\cdots+(n-1)\), and \((n-1,n-2,\dots,0)\) seems much larger because it starts with the biggest digit. I still don’t see how that helps prove every good reversal decreases something, though.
exchanges.tutor: Good, that's exactly right. Now focus on the simplest case: suppose you do a good reversal on just two elements — say a_i > a_j with i < j. What happens to the base-n value when you swap those two digits?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly understood the base-n representation. Now I should guide them to connect a 'good reversal' to what happens to this number. The key insight is that a good reversal swaps a larger digit in a higher position with a smaller digit - which should decrease the base-n value. I'll ask them to think about what happens to the base-n number specifically when you swap just two elements where the earlier one is larger.

exchanges.student: I think it should go down, because a bigger digit moves to the right and a smaller digit moves to the left. But for a full reversal with lots of middle terms, I lose track fast, so could you maybe just explain the whole proof idea for part (c) in one shot?
exchanges.tutor: I won't give you the full proof — that's yours to write! But you're on the right track. Here's a nudge: in a full reversal from index i to j, what happens to the middle terms (the ones between i and j)? Do they move to a higher or lower position in the number?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right intuition for the two-element case and is now asking me to just give them the full proof. I must refuse per the rules - never give the answer directly. Instead, I should acknowledge their correct insight and guide them to extend it to the full reversal case by asking a focused question about the middle terms.

exchanges.student: That’s where I’m stuck, because some middle terms move left and some move right, so it feels like they could cancel in a messy way. I can’t tell whether I’m supposed to compare term-by-term, use some inequality, or do a totally different argument, and I’m kind of mixing all of that up.
exchanges.tutor: Don't worry about the middle terms for a moment. Focus on just positions i and j: since a_i > a_j and i < j, what is the net change in the base-n value from swapping just those two digits? Is it positive or negative?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is overthinking the middle terms. The key insight is that you don't need to track middle terms at all - in a reversal, the middle terms just swap positions with each other (they get reversed among themselves), so their combined contribution to the base-n value might change in a complex way. However, the cleaner approach is to think about what the hint really suggests: the entire base-n number strictly decreases. I should guide them to think about which digit positions are actually being compared - specifically the outermost pair (i and j) - and whether that alone is enough to determine the direction of change, without worrying about the middle.

exchanges.student: I think the change would be negative if I do new minus old, since the bigger digit loses a bigger place value and the smaller digit gains one. But I’m still worried that in the actual reversal, the middle digits could outweigh that, and I don’t know a clean way to show they don’t.
exchanges.tutor: Think about the structure of the reversal more carefully. When you reverse positions i through j, which pairs of elements actually get swapped with each other? Is every such pair also an inverted pair?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still worried about the middle terms. The key insight they're missing is that the middle terms in a reversal are themselves reversed - meaning they form pairs that swap with each other. Each such interior pair (a_{i+k}, a_{j-k}) also gets swapped. So the total change is actually a sum over ALL swapped pairs in the reversal, not just the outermost pair. This is the critical structural insight - every pair in the reversal is itself an inverted pair (because we chose a good reversal). I should guide them to see this.

exchanges.student: I’m not sure every mirrored pair has to be inverted, and that’s making me doubt the whole direction. Maybe I’m overcomplicating it, but could you just give me a broader explanation of how to think about part (c) before I get lost in the details again?
exchanges.tutor: You're right that not every interior pair is necessarily inverted — good catch. Let me refocus you. After a reversal on (i, j), what digit ends up at position i (the leftmost changed position)? How does it compare to a_i, and why does that position matter most in the base-n number?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is right to doubt that every interior pair is necessarily inverted - that's actually not guaranteed, and I should acknowledge that. The cleaner approach is actually simpler: you don't need every interior pair to be inverted. You just need the total base-n value to decrease. The student already established the outermost swap causes a strict decrease. The key insight is that the base-n value is bounded below by 0 (it's always non-negative) and is always an integer, so I should redirect them toward simply confirming: (1) is the base-n value always a non-negative integer? and (2) does a good reversal strictly decrease it? For (2), rather than tracking all pairs, maybe the derived variable isn't the base-n number itself but something else. Let me reconsider - the hint says to think of the permutation as digits of a base-n number. The natural derived variable is the base-n number itself. A good reversal on (i,j) with a_i > a_j: the change is (a_j - a_i)(n^{n-1-i} - n^{n-1-j}). Since a_j < a_i, (a_j - a_i) < 0, and since i < j, n^{n-1-i} > n^{n-1-j}, so the product is negative. The middle terms: they get reversed, so each interior pair (i+k, j-k) swaps. The change from each such swap is (a_{j-k} - a_{i+k})(n^{n-1-(i+k)} - n^{n-1-(j-k)}). This could be positive or negative depending on whether a_{i+k} > a_{j-k} or not. So the total change is not necessarily negative from all pairs! This means the base-n number is NOT necessarily a valid derived variable. Wait - let me reconsider the problem. The hint says base-n number. Let me think again... Actually, I think I need to reconsider. Maybe the derived variable is NOT the base-n number of the permutation itself, but rather the base-n number formed by treating the permutation differently, or perhaps it's the base-n number and it DOES always decrease. Let me think about the example given: (9,1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,0) → (0,8,7,6,5,4,3,2,1,9). The base-10 number: 9123456780 → 0876543219 = 876543219. Yes, it decreased! But we said inverted pairs increased from 17 to 28. So the base-n number did decrease even though inverted pairs increased. Now, the question is whether it ALWAYS decreases. The reversal from i to j reverses the subsequence. The change to the base-n value is: sum over all pairs (i+k, j-k) that get swapped of (a_{j-k} - a_{i+k})(n^{n-1-(i+k)} - n^{n-1-(j-k)}). For the outermost pair k=0: (a_j - a_i)(n^{n-1-i} - n^{n-1-j}) < 0 since a_j < a_i and n^{n-1-i} > n^{n-1-j}. For interior pairs: (a_{j-k} - a_{i+k})(n^{n-1-i-k} - n^{n-1-j+k}). The second factor n^{n-1-i-k} - n^{n-1-j+k} could be positive or negative depending on whether i+k < j-k or not, i.e., whether i+j > 2k or not... Hmm, this is getting complicated. Actually wait - I think the key insight the problem wants is that we should use a DIFFERENT derived variable inspired by the base-n hint but not necessarily the base-n number itself. OR, perhaps the derived variable IS the base-n number and we need to prove it decreases by a different argument. Actually, I recall that for this type of problem, the standard approach is: the derived variable is the base-n number N(σ) = Σ a_i * n^{n-1-i}. To show it strictly decreases under any good reversal, the argument goes: a good reversal on (i,j) with a_i > a_j changes the value by Σ_{k=0}^{floor((j-i-1)/2)} (a_{j-k} - a_{i+k})(n^{n-1-(i+k)} - n^{n-1-(j-k)}). For each k, n^{n-1-(i+k)} > n^{n-1-(j-k)} iff i+k < j-k iff k < (j-i)/2. But we also need a_{j-k} - a_{i+k} to have a consistent sign. It doesn't necessarily. Hmm. So maybe the argument needs to use the fact that n^{n-1-i} - n^{n-1-j} >> sum of all other changes? Let's think: the change from the outermost pair is (a_j-a_i)(n^{n-1-i} - n^{n-1-j}) ≤ -(n^{n-1-i} - n^{n-1-j}) (since a_i - a_j ≥ 1). The maximum positive contribution from interior swaps: each interior pair contributes at most (n-1)(n^{n-1-(i+1)} - n^{n-1-(j-1)}) in absolute value... Actually the total absolute change from ALL interior pairs is bounded by (n-1) * Σ_{k=1}^{...} (n^{n-2-i} - ...) < (n-1) * n^{n-2-i} * n = n^{n-1-i} * (1 - something). Actually let me think differently. The maximum possible positive change from all interior swaps combined: each interior element a_{i+k} or a_{j-k} is at most n-1, so the maximum increase from any swap of interior pair k is (n-1) * (n^{n-1-(i+k)} + n^{n-1-(j-k)}) ... no this isn't right either. Let me think about whether the outermost swap change dominates. The change from swapping (i,j) alone: (a_j - a_i)(n^{n-1-i} - n^{n-1-j}) ≤ -(n^{n-1-i} - n^{n-1-j}) = -(n^{n-1-j})(n^{j-i} - 1). The maximum positive change from all interior swaps: each pair (i+k, j-k) for k=1,...: the change is (a_{j-k}-a_{i+k})(n^{n-1-i-k} - n^{n-1-j+k}). The maximum of |a_{j-k}-a_{i+k}| * |n^{n-1-i-k} - n^{n-1-j+k}| ≤ (n-1) * n^{n-1-i-k} (when i+k < j-k). Summing over k=1 to floor((j-i)/2)-ish: ≤ (n-1) * Σ_{k=1}^∞ n^{n-2-i-k} ... hmm actually I think this approach works but it's messy. Actually, I think the cleaner approach (and what the problem likely intends) is: The base-n value N = Σ a_i n^{n-1-i}. The key observation: since the digits a_i are all distinct values in {0,...,n-1} (it's a permutation), and n is the base, the base-n number formed by any permutation is an integer between 0 and n^n - 1... but more importantly: A good reversal on (i,j) means a_i > a_j. The LEADING change (from position i) is that a_i gets replaced by some value ≤ a_i (namely a_j), so the most significant changed digit decreases. This means the overall base-n number decreases, regardless of what happens to less significant digits! This is the key: in the reversal, position i gets digit a_j < a_i. All other changed positions (i+1, ..., j) are LESS significant (lower powers of n). So the decrease of at least 1 at position i (the most significant changed position) outweighs any possible increase at less significant positions. More precisely: The change at position i is (a_j - a_i) * n^{n-1-i} ≤ -n^{n-1-i} (since a_i - a_j ≥ 1). The maximum possible increase from all other positions i+1,...,j: Σ_{k=i+1}^{j} (n-1) * n^{n-1-k} ≤ (n-1) * Σ_{k=i+1}^{∞} n^{n-1-k} = (n-1) * n^{n-2-i} * 1/(1-1/n) = (n-1) * n^{n-2-i} * n/(n-1) = n^{n-1-i}. So the maximum total increase from positions i+1,...,j is strictly less than n^{n-1-i} (it's ≤ n^{n-1-i} - n^{n-1-j} < n^{n-1-i}). Therefore: total change ≤ -n^{n-1-i} + (n^{n-1-i} - n^{n-1-j}) = -n^{n-1-j} < 0. This is the clean proof! The derived variable is N(σ) = Σ a_i * n^{n-1-i}, which is always a non-negative integer, and every good reversal decreases it by at least n^{n-1-j} ≥ 1. So it strictly decreases. This is the argument I should guide the student toward. The key insight: position i is the MOST significant position that changes, and digit a_i strictly decreases there, which dominates everything else. I should guide the student to this insight without giving it away. I'll ask them to think about what happens specifically at position i after the reversal.

exchanges.student: At position \(i\), the new digit is \(a_j\), and since it’s a good reversal I know \(a_j<a_i\). I guess that matters most because position \(i\) is the highest-place-value spot among all the positions that changed, but I still want help turning that into a full argument instead of just a hint.
exchanges.tutor: Great, that's the key insight. Now try to bound it: what is the maximum possible total increase in the base-n value from positions i+1 through j? Think about the geometric series Σ_{k=i+1}^{∞} (n-1)·n^{n-1-k}.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the crucial insight: position i gets a smaller digit and it's the most significant changed position. Now I need to guide them to quantify this: the decrease at position i is at least n^{n-1-i}, and the maximum possible increase from all other changed positions (i+1 through j) is strictly less than n^{n-1-i}. I should ask them to bound the maximum possible increase from the remaining changed positions.

exchanges.student: I’m getting lost again with that bound and the indexing. Could you just walk me through the whole argument cleanly, including how the geometric-series bound fits, because I can’t see the structure from these pieces.
